name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (65)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى سَفَاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٦٦)
```

```english
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘Indeed we see you to be in folly, and indeed we consider you to be a liar.’ (66)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى سَفَاهَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in folly. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (67)
```

```arabic
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَاصِحٌ أَمِينٌ (٦٨)
```

```english
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord and I am a trustworthy well-wisher for you. (68)
```

```arabic
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَزَادَكُمْ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ بَصْۜطَةً ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Do you consider it odd that there should come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among yourselves, so that he may warn you? Remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and increased you vastly in creation. So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’ (69)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِنَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَحْدَهُۥ وَنَذَرَ مَا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا ۖ فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٧٠)
```

```english
They said, ‘Have you come to [tell] us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers have been worshiping? Then bring us what you threaten us with, should you be truthful.’ (70)
```

```arabic
قَالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ ۖ أَتُجَـٰدِلُونَنِى فِىٓ أَسْمَآءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ (٧١)
```

```english
He said, ‘Punishment and wrath from your Lord has become due against you. Do you dispute with me regarding names, which you have named—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority? So wait! I too am waiting along with you.’ (71)
```

```arabic
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَقَطَعْنَا دَابِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۖ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٢)
```

```english
Then We delivered him and those who were with him by a mercy from Us, and We rooted out those who denied Our signs and were not faithful. (72)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ** "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah...’" until the end of the verse. [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:65]

**The brother** (الأخ) — originally *akhuw* — is one who shares with another in birth ontologically (تكويناً) from the one who begot them, whether the shared parent is the father, the mother, or both. It may also be according to a divine law (شرع إلهي), such as a brother through fosterage (الأخ الرضاعي), or a social custom (سنة اجتماعية), such as a brother by adoption (الأخ بالدعاء) as was practiced by certain peoples. This is its primary meaning. Subsequently, it was used metaphorically for anyone who belongs to a people (قوم), a town (بلدة), a craft (صنعة), or a disposition (سجية), and the like. It is said: "a brother of the Banu Tamim," "a brother of Yathrib," "a brother of weaving," and "a brother of generosity." It is from this category that His saying occurs: **وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً** "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother."

The discussion regarding His saying: **قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ** "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:65] is identical to the discussion regarding the similar address in the previous story. If you ask: Why was the conjunction (العاطف) omitted from His saying **قالَ يا قَوْمِ** "He said, ‘O my people...’" and He did not say: "So he said" (فقال) as in the story of Noah? I say: It is based on an implied question. It is as if when He said: **وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً** "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother," it was asked: "So what did Hud say?" Thus, it was answered: "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah...’" This was stated by al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf*.

This reasoning does not apply to the story of Noah because it was the first story mentioned. This story, however, is one story following another, wherein the mind of the listener is prepared to ask after having grasped the summary of the narrative and knowing that the story of sending a messenger involves a call (دعوة), a rejection (رد), and an acceptance (قبول). Thus, it was fitting that when the listener heard His saying: **وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً** "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother," he would ask: "What did Hud say to his people?" And the answer is: "He said to them..." (etc.).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ** "The elite of the faithless among his people said..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:66] until the end of the verse. Since there were those among this elite who believed in Allah but concealed their faith—as will come later in the story—unlike the elite among the people of Noah, He said here in the story of Hud: **قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ** "The elite of the faithless among his people said," whereas He said in the story of Noah: **قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ** "The elite of his people said." This was mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. Regarding His saying, the Exalted, narrating their words: **إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِي سَفاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ** "‘Indeed we see you in folly, and we indeed consider you to be a liar’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:66], they emphasized their speech repeatedly because they heard from him a statement they never expected anyone to utter. Their gods had taken their place in their hearts, and the custom of idolatry (الوثنية) had become so firmly established among them that no one dared to object to it. Thus, they marveled at his statement and rejected it with an expression of wonder. They confronted him first by claiming he was in folly (سفاهة) — which is a lightness of intellect (خفة العقل) that leads to errors in judgment — and secondly, by stating that they held a very strong suspicion that he was among the liars. It is as if by "the liars" they were referring to their prophets, for the idolaters would not submit to prophethood (النبوة), and prophets had come to them before Hud, as the Exalted mentions in His saying: **وَتِلْكَ عادٌ جَحَدُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا رُسُلَهُ** "Such were ‘Ad: they denied the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles..." (Hud: 59). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 11:59]

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ** "He said, ‘O my people, there is no folly in me...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:67]. The discussion of this verse is similar to the discussion of its counterpart in the story of Noah, except that ‘Ad increased in insolence (وقاحة) over the people of Noah. While the latter accused Noah of error (الضلال) in judgment, the former accused Hud of folly (السفاهة). Yet, Hud did not abandon the dignity (وقار) of prophethood, nor did he forget the required etiquette (أدب) of the divine call. He answered them by saying: **يا قَوْمِ** "O my people," showing his affection for them and his eagerness for their salvation, **لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ وَلكِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "‘there is no folly in me; rather I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:67] He proceeded by stripping his speech of all emphasis, sufficing with a mere rebuttal of their accusation and an affirmation of the messengership he claimed, to indicate its self-evidence (ظهور).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنَا لَكُمْ ناصِحٌ أَمِينٌ** "‘I deliver to you the messages of my Lord, and I am a trustworthy advisor for you.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:68] That is: "I have no concern, insofar as I am a messenger, except to deliver the messages of my Lord, pure from the taint of what you suspect of me being a liar. I am not deceptive (غاش) toward you in what I wish to enjoin upon you, nor am I treacherous (خائن) regarding the truth I possess by altering it, nor regarding your rights by neglecting them. What I desire from you—adherence to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — is what I see as the truth, and it is that which contains your benefit and your good." He described himself as "trustworthy" (الأمين) as a counterpoint to their saying: **وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ** "‘and we indeed consider you to be a liar.’"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَنْ جاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** "‘Do you marvel that a reminder from your Lord has come to you...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:69] until the end of the verse. The word *basṭah* (البصطة) is the same as *basṭah* (البسطة) — *physical stature or amplitude* — where the letter *sīn* (س) is changed to *ṣād* (ص) due to its proximity to the letter *ṭā’* (ط), which is one of the letters of velarization (الإطباق), similar to *al-ṣirāṭ* (الصراط) and *al-sirāṭ* (السراط). **The bounties** (الآلاء) is the plural of *ila* (إلى), with the *hamzah* being either *fatḥah* or *kasrah*, meaning blessing (النعمة), like *ānā’* (آناء) is the plural of *anā* (أنى) or *inā* (إنى).

Then he (peace be upon him) denounced their wonder at his messengership to them, similar to what was previously mentioned regarding Noah (peace be upon him). He reminded them of Allah's blessings upon them, specifically highlighting two manifest blessings: that Allah made them successors (خلفاء) on the earth after Noah, and that Allah distinguished them among the nations with an amplitude of creation (بسطة الخلق) and a greatness of physical stature (عظم الهيكل البدني) which necessitated increased strength and power. From this, it appears that they were a people of civilization (حضارة), progress (تقدم), and renown in valor and might. He then followed these two by referring to all other blessings in His saying, the Exalted: **فَاذْكُرُوا آلاءَ اللهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** "‘So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:69]

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِنَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَحْدَهُ وَنَذَرَ ما كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا** "They said, ‘Have you come to us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers used to worship?’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:70] until the end of the verse. In this is their adherence to the imitation (تقليد) of their forefathers, and an attempt to incapacitate Hud, mixed with a type of mockery (استهزاء) regarding the punishment he warned them of.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ** "He said, ‘Punishment and wrath from your Lord have already become due against you...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71] until the end of the verse. **The abomination** (الرجس) and **the punishment** (الرجز) refer to a matter which, when it befalls a thing, necessitates its distancing or being distanced from. Therefore, it is applied to filth (القاذورة) because a human being feels aversion and moves away from it, and to punishment (العذاب) because the punished one (المعذب) — as a passive participle — is distanced from the one who punishes him or from people who are safe from the punishment.

He answered them that their persistence in worshipping idols through the imitation of their forefathers necessitated that distancing from Allah through abomination (الرجس) and wrath (الغضب) become due upon them. Then, he branched from this by threatening them with the punishment they were seeking to hasten, informing them of its inevitable descent upon them. He alluded to this by commanding them to wait and informing them that he, like them, was waiting for the descent of the punishment, saying: **فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ** "‘So wait! I too am waiting along with you.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71]

As for his saying: **أَتُجادِلُونَنِي فِي أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ** "‘Do you dispute with me regarding names which you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority?’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71], it is a rebuttal of what they relied upon regarding the divinity of their gods—namely, that they found their forefathers worshipping them, and they considered their forefathers more perfect and more intelligent than themselves or those of their own rank like Hud, thus believing they must imitate them.

The gist of it is that you and your forefathers are equal in that you have all produced things for which you have no authority (سلطان) — which is a proof (البرهان) and a decisive argument (الحجة القاطعة) — for the attribute of divinity you claim for them. Thus, nothing remains of divinity for them except the names (الأسماء) you have called them by, when you said: "god of fertility," "god of war," "god of the sea," and "god of the land." These names have no referents (مصاديق) except in your imaginations. So, do you dispute with me regarding names? A human being may name anything whatever he wishes, provided he does not consider the meaning to be realized in external reality (الخارج).

The Quran has repeatedly used this reasoning to demonstrate the falsehood of idolatry: **أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ** "‘names which you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority.’" This is among the most subtle and refined of explanations, and the most eloquent and decisive of arguments. For if a person does not provide a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the claim he makes, nothing remains of the attribute he claims except the naming (التسمية) and the expression (التعبير). It is the height of ignorance for a person to rely on such an illusory attribute.

This explanation extends and applies, through analysis, to all instances where a human being places his trust in causes (الأسباب) other than Allah, the Glorified, and grants them a degree of independence (استقلال) that necessitates his heart's attachment to them, his obedience to them, and his seeking proximity to them. For Allah, the Glorified, has in several instances in His speech considered the obedience to other than Him and the reliance upon those besides Him as worship (عبادة) of them. He said: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** "‘Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me...”’" (Ya Sin: 60-61). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 36:60-61]

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فأَنْجَيْناهُ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا** "So We delivered him and those who were with him by a mercy from Us..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:72] until the end of the verse. The use of the indefinite form for "mercy" (رحمة) is to indicate its specific type—that is, a particular kind of mercy, which is the mercy reserved for the believers in the form of the victory promised to them. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ** "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (al-Mu’min: 51) [QARAI TRANSLATION: 40:51], and He said: **وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "...and it was a must for Us to help the believers" (al-Rum: 47). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 30:47]

And His saying: **وَقَطَعْنا دابِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا** "‘...and We cut off the roots of those who denied Our signs...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:72] is an allusion (كناية) to their destruction and the severing of their progeny. For **the root/remnant** (الدابر) is that which follows a thing from behind. Sometimes it describes a matter preceding a thing, such as "the passing yesterday" (أمس الدابر), and sometimes it describes what follows, such as the "rear of the people" (دابر القوم), which refers to those at the very end of them. The act of "cutting off" (القطع) is attributed to the "root/remnant" (الدابر) with the consideration that the subsequent progeny is a "follower" (دابر) connected to the person through an extended cause (سبب ممتد). Thus, the destruction of a person in this manner is as if this connected cause between him and his progeny has been severed.

The detailed discussion regarding the story of Hud (peace be upon him) will come in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Hud, if Allah wills.
